Secondly, when Ibrahim (AS) asked if his descendants would also receive this role, Allah's reply made it clear what kind of person can receive this promise. He said: قَالَ لَا يَنَالُ عَهْدِي الظَّالِمِينَ - “My covenant does not include the wrongdoers.” Here, the term ظَالِم (wrongdoer) in تاویل is a profound word with a deep, hidden meaning that extends beyond someone who sins. It refers to a spiritually dark soul, fundamentally out of tune with Allah's perfect order. The primary manifestation of this darkness, this ظلم , occurs when someone attempts to assume the spiritual position of Imamat that they don't deserve because they are trying to place a dark soul where a soul of light ( نُور ) should be. Imagine a special torch designed by a brilliant inventor to shine a unique, pure light. This torch is meant to be passed down through a family. Still, the inventor makes a rule: only those who are specifically chosen by him, and whose internal essence is perfectly pure, can carry and operate this torch effectively. The Torch: This torch is like the Imamate, a divine covenant ( عَهْد ) from Allah. It carries a unique, pure light ( نُور ), which is the divine guidance. The Inventor's Rule: When the inventor says, "My covenant does not include the wrongdoers ( ظَالِمِينَ )," he means that anyone whose internal essence is flawed, or who tries to steal or grab the torch without being properly designated by the inventor, cannot truly carry its pure light. They might hold the physical torch, but it won't shine properly in their hands. It will eventually sputter and die because the inner essence of the one holding it is not compatible with its pure function. Therefore, the "wrongdoer" here isn't just someone who makes a mistake or sins. It's someone whose very nature or action is fundamentally misaligned with the purpose of the torch – someone who tries to usurp the role without the required inner purity and designation from the inventor. This process of Imamat, transmitting from father to son, ensures that the divine promise of light and guidance remains unbroken throughout all time. It's not nepotism; it's divine wisdom. This entire journey demonstrates how Allah fulfilled His promise to reveal to Ibrahim (AS) the spiritual dominion ( مَلَكُوت ) of reality. By successfully navigating these earthly levels of guidance ( مَلَكُوت الْأَرْض ), he became worthy of understanding the celestial truths ( مَلَكُوت السَّمَاوَات ) and reached a state of absolute, unwavering faith.

title: The Conversation With Azar
text: Let's examine the story of Ibrahim (AS) conversing with his "father" and breaking idols. These are fantastic examples teaching us about the difference between a static, inherited faith and a living, intellectual faith. The Spiritual Father and the Living Invitation When the Quran narrates that Ibrahim (AS) is speaking with his "father" ( أَب ), it is not referring to his biological father, Azar. Think of "father" here as his primary teacher, the elder who raised him in the common religious traditions of their time. We know from the tradition of Rasul Allah (SWS) that all the prophets come from a pure family line of true believers, so it wouldn't make sense for the biological father of Ibrahim (AS) to be an idol worshipper. Azar represents the leader of the established religious system of Nuh at the time. Similarly, the "idols" he worshipped were not just statues made of wood or stone. Those "idols" are a symbol for rigid ideas and beliefs that people follow without thinking. They are like statues because they're lifeless—they can't answer questions, offer any guidance, or provide spiritual energy. They are upheld by scholars who may hold high ranks but are spiritually closed off to living truths. So, when Allah granted Ibrahim (AS) special, divine knowledge, he went back to his teacher. He wasn't being rude; he was trying to help him level up. Imagine you learn basic addition from your first-grade teacher. Years later, you become a scientist who understands the whole universe through complex physics. If you go back to your old teacher, you might say, "What you taught me was the perfect start, but I've found a deeper way to see things. Let me show you." That's precisely what Ibrahim (AS) was doing. He was inviting his teacher to move beyond blind copying and to embrace a living faith, guided by fresh knowledge from Allah. Mary (19:42) ‏ ‏إذْ قَالَ لأَبيه يَأَبَت لمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لَا يَسْمَعُ وَلَا يُبْصرُ وَلَا يُغْنى عَنكَ شَيْـًۭٔا ‎ ‎ Translation: [Mention] when he said to his father, "O my father, why do you worship that which does not hear and does not see and will not benefit you at all? This "straight path" ( صِرَطًا سَوِيًّا ), he mentions, is the timeless path of divine guidance that, in every age, leads a person to recognize the living Imam of his Time. It’s a kind call to leave behind old, rusty ideas and connect with the living knowledge available right now.

title: The Four Birds
text: The Quranic story of Ibrahim (AS) asking Allah to show him how He brings life to the dead is a request for a deeper understanding, not because he doubted Allah, but because he wanted to witness spiritual revival directly. He sought to understand how a human soul can be transformed from the "death" of not knowing (ignorance) and being focused solely on worldly things, to the true "life" of spiritual Gnosis ( مَعْرِفَة ), which is a profound, intuitive understanding of Allah. The Parable of the Four Birds Allah's response to Ibrahim (AS)'s question is a living parable for how His divine mission ( الدعوة ) is established and how souls are awakened: The Cow (2:260) ‏ ‏وَإذْ قَالَ إبْرَهیمُ رَبّ أَرنى كَيْفَ تُحْى ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۖ قَالَ أَوَلَمْ تُؤْمن ۖ قَالَ بَلَىٰ وَلَكن لّيَطْمَئنَّ قَلْبى ۖ قَالَ فَخُذْ أَرْبَعَةًۭ مّنَ ٱلطَّيْر فَصُرْهُنَّ إلَيْكَ ثُمَّ ٱجْعَلْ عَلَىٰ كُلّ جَبَلٍۢ مّنْهُنَّ جُزْءًۭا ثُمَّ ٱدْعُهُنَّ يَأْتينَكَ سَعْيًۭا وَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزيزٌ حَكيمٌۭ ‎ ‎ Translation: And [mention] when Abraham said, "My Lord, show me how You give life to the dead." [Allah] said, "Have you not believed?" He said, "Yes, but [I ask] only that my heart may be satisfied." [Allah] said, "Take four birds and commit them to yourself. Then [after slaughtering them] put on each hill a portion of them; then call them - they will come [flying] to you in haste. And know that Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise." Ibrahim (AS) asks Allah, رَبِّ أَرِنِى كَيْفَ تُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ Master, show me how you bring dead souls back to life? Allah asks: قَالَ أَوَلَمْ تُؤْمِن . Do you not believe? Ibrahim (AS) clarifies his motives by saying: قَالَ بَلَىٰ وَلَكِن لِّيَطْمَئِنَّ قَلْبِى, "Yes, but [I ask] only so that my heart becomes reassured." He had the faith that comes from knowledge ( عِلْمُ الْيَقِين ), but he sought the certainty of direct vision ( عَيْنُ الْيَقِين ). It's like knowing from a blueprint that an engine should work versus seeing and hearing it roar to life. He wanted to witness the beautiful system through which Allah grants the soul true life. In response, Allah commanded him to take four birds, tame them, and make them incline towards him. Birds symbolize pure souls, as they can ascend towards the heavens. These four birds represent four pure human souls, who are among the highest ranks of the spiritual hierarchy ( حُدُود الدِّين ) and are ready to receive divine knowledge directly from the Imam. They are like his chief deputies or Proofs ( ابواب ). The command to tame these birds symbolizes the intense spiritual training ( تَرْبِيَة ) and discipline that the Imam provides to his deputies. He shares his knowledge with them until their hearts and minds are perfectly aligned with his own, becoming perfect reflections of his wisdom. Ibrahim (AS) was then told to place each bird on a separate "mountain" ( جَبَل ). A mountain here is not just a physical peak; it's a symbol of a significant region or a community of faith ( جَزِيرَة ) in the world. Placing a trained deputy on a "mountain" means appointing these four حجۃ as the head of the spiritual mission in that land, making them a firm and elevated source of guidance for its people. When Ibrahim (AS) calls to them, they return to him instantly, "in haste." This shows the absolute power and unity of the Imam's spiritual call. No matter how far away they are physically, his trained deputies are in perfect, immediate spiritual connection with him. His call is a spiritual reality that goes beyond space and time, and their response is immediate and specific because they are extensions of his own spiritual will. Practical Example: Think about it this way. Imagine a highly skilled CEO with a clear vision for their company. They don't run every single branch themselves. Instead, they choose four highly trusted and capable regional managers (the four birds) and train them personally and extensively (taming the birds) until these managers fully understand the CEO's vision and methods. Then, the CEO assigns each manager to a different, important region (mountain). When the CEO makes a crucial decision or issues a strategic directive, these managers instantly understand and implement it, even across vast distances, because they are so perfectly aligned with the CEO's will and vision. This parable is Allah’s answer to Ibrahim (AS), demonstrating how He gives life to the "dead" souls: He does so through His chosen guide, the Imam, who trains and sends out his representatives throughout the world. These deputies, carrying the Imam's call, awaken the souls of humanity, reviving them with the eternal life of knowledge and faith. This is the living, breathing proof of Allah's system for the salvation of humankind.

title: Breaking The Idols
text: The well-known story of Ibrahim (AS) illustrates how, during a festival when people were away celebrating, Ibrahim (AS) seized a brave opportunity to make a significant statement. When Ibrahim (AS) was sure that everyone had left town, he entered the temple and caught sight of the various idols made of wood and stone that were worshiped by the townsfolk. He also noticed that food had been placed before the idols as offerings by the people. He challenges them as the Quran states: Those Who Set the Ranks (37:92) ‏ ‏مَا لَكُمْ لَا تَنطقُونَ ‎ ‎ Translation: What is [wrong] with you that you do not speak? " He then broke all the idols except the largest one, hanging the axe on its shoulder to demonstrate its helplessness. His goal was for the people to realize that their idols couldn’t be true gods because they were powerless. When they returned and saw the destroyed statues, they became angry and suspected Ibrahim (AS), bringing him before the assembly to determine who was responsible. Ibrahim (AS) confidently declared, The Prophets (21:63) ‏ ‏قَالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُ كَبيرُهُمْ هَذَا فَسْـَٔلُوهُمْ إن كَانُوا۟ يَنطقُونَ ‎ ‎ Translation: He said, "Rather, this - the largest of them - did it, so ask them, if they should [be able to] speak." He challenged their logic, questioning how a large, immovable statue could break smaller ones, and if it couldn't defend itself, how it could be considered a god. The crowd was left speechless as they recognized their idols' helplessness and understood they had been wrong to worship them. However, their pride and stubbornness soon led them to plan severe punishment for Ibrahim (AS). ESOTERICS This parable is about the power of the intellect ( عَقْل ) and the use of spiritual proof to break concepts. Here, the idols represent the weak arguments of the religious authorities. When Ibrahim (AS) "breaks" them, he employs powerful logic to shatter their incorrect beliefs and demonstrate that they have nothing substantial to offer. His move to leave the biggest idol standing was a genius debate tactic. That "largest idol" refers to their most prominent scholar or highest religious authority of the time. By challenging them to ask this main idol what happened, he put them in a corner. Think about it like this: Imagine trying to understand how your smartphone works by only using an encyclopedia from the 1950s. That old book is the "idol." It contains information, but it's completely outdated and irrelevant to your question. The act of Ibrahim (AS) is like a modern engineer showing everyone the smartphone and saying, "Go ahead, ask your old encyclopedia to explain this." Of course, it can't. When their biggest "idol" or top scholar couldn't provide an answer, everyone could see that his authority was empty. The lesson for us is that true faith is built on reason and proof, not on just accepting what someone says, especially if they can't rationally explain their own beliefs. It encourages us to seek a faith that makes sense.

title: The Debate With The King
text: News of this miracle reached the proud king of the land, Nimrod. He summoned Ibrahim (AS) to his palace. Nimrod, full of pride, claimed he was the lord of the world. The Quran describes the debate in Surah Baqarah: The Cow (2:258) ‏ ‏أَلَمْ تَرَ إلَى ٱلَّذى حَاجَّ إبْرَهیمَ فى رَبّه أَنْ آتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُلْكَ إذْ قَالَ إبْرَهیمُ رَبّىَ ٱلَّذى يُحْى وَيُميتُ قَالَ أَنَا۠ أُحْى وَأُميتُ ۖ قَالَ إبْرَهیمُ فَإنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْتى بٱلشَّمْس منَ ٱلْمَشْرق فَأْت بهَا منَ ٱلْمَغْرب فَبُهتَ ٱلَّذى كَفَرَ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّلمينَ ‎ ‎ Translation: Have you not considered the one who argued with Abraham about his Lord [merely] because Allah had given him kingship? When Abraham said, "My Lord is the one who gives life and causes death," he said, "I give life and cause death." Abraham said, "Indeed, Allah brings up the sun from the east, so bring it up from the west." So the disbeliever was overwhelmed [by astonishment], and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people. The king tries to show off his power by making a shallow claim: أَنا أُحْى وَأُميتُ "I give life and cause death." Essentially, he can pardon a prisoner (granting "life") or execute someone (causing "death"). Ibrahim (AS) sees through this, recognizing it as worldly power, not true, creative power. He shifts the debate to a cosmic level, presenting a proof no earthly king can match. He states that Allah makes the sun rise from the east and challenges the king to make it rise from the west. Here, the " sun " symbolizes the Word of Allah and the light of revelation . This spiritual "sun" rises from the "east" with a Prophet ( نَاطِق ), illuminating the world as it moves toward the "west," where its authority is entrusted to his successor, the Imam ( أَسَاس ). Confronted with this cosmic proof of a power beyond his own, the king is intellectually shattered and defeated. This exemplifies the unchangeable Divine System ( سُنَّةُ اللهِ ) governing humanity's guidance, which no earthly king can alter. How This Was a Total Defeat Nimrod's defeat was not physical but an intellectual and spiritual annihilation. Ibrahim (AS) exposed the limits of Nimrod's power and the emptiness of his claim to be a "lord." When Ibrahim (AS) challenged Nimrod, "Allah brings the sun from the east, so you bring it from the west," he made a profound spiritual point: claiming divine power is futile if one cannot influence the divine system. To demonstrate divine authority truly, one must reverse the flow of spiritual history—make the sun of revelation rise from the West instead of the East, and reveal inner meaning before outer words. This challenge was impossible for Nimrod and thus devastating. It was a checkmate. Here’s how it defeated him: It revealed his actual limit: by using the unchangeable cosmic law of guidance as proof, Ibrahim (AS) showed Nimrod had no power in the spiritual realm—the only realm that truly matters for guidance and salvation. It exposed his claim as empty: Nimrod claimed to be a "lord" ( رَبّ ), a sustainer of reality, but couldn't grasp or alter the spiritual truth that governs mankind's souls. He was a king of dust and flesh, but powerless over the spiritual sun. It caused intellectual paralysis: the Quran uses ( فَبُهِتَ ), meaning he was dumbfounded, bewildered, and overwhelmed—a shock that shatters entire worldviews. He had no argument left, having built his identity on being the ultimate power, but Ibrahim (AS) showed him a power beyond his own, leaving him speechless and paralyzed. The defeat was not about failing a magical trick. Still, Nimrod was forced to confront the fact that his worldly kingdom was a tiny, insignificant speck compared to Allah's vast, unchangeable spiritual kingdom, whose laws he could never challenge. He was shown to be a king of the physical world but a complete subject in the world of the spirit.

title: Trial By Fire
text: Next, we see what happens when those who rely solely on blind faith encounter clear logic and reason. After Ibrahim (AS) demonstrated that the idols were powerless, the literalist teachers and leaders were left speechless, unable to produce a valid response. They were effectively defeated. Not only were these individuals humbled, but even the powerful king of the time was brought to a state of intellectual standstill. When such people in positions of power lose an argument, their vulnerability often manifests as anger and violence. Instead of admitting they were wrong, they chose to attack. Their pride and rage overwhelmed them, leading to loud protests. So naturally, by the command of Nimrood, the Quran says they demanded to burn him. The Prophets (21:68) ‏ ‏قَالُوا۟ حَرّقُوهُ وَٱنصُرُوا۟ آلهَتَكُمْ إن كُنتُمْ فَعلينَ ‎ ‎ Translation: They said, "Burn him and defend your gods - if you are to act." They decided to make an example of him by burning him at the stake. They built a fire that is reported to be no ordinary fire. It was enormous, a colossal blaze so vast and scorching hot that no one could even get near it. The heat was too intense to walk up and throw him in simply. To solve this problem, they constructed a massive machine, a catapult, known in tradition as a ( منجنیق ). This powerful device was built for one purpose: to hurl Ibrahim (AS) from a great distance into the very heart of the inferno they had created. They seized Ibrahim (AS), and as he placed his complete and perfect trust in Allah, they launched him from the catapult. He was sent flying through the air, heading directly for the raging flames. At that very moment, as he soared towards what seemed like certain death, Allah intervened directly. He issued a command not to the people, but to the fire itself. It was a command that changed the very nature of creation for his beloved Prophet: The Prophets (21:69) ‏ ‏قُلْنَا يَنَارُ كُونى بَرْدًۭا وَسَلَمًا عَلَىٰ إبْرَهيمَ ‎ ‎ Translation: Allah said, "O fire, be coolness and safety upon Abraham." "O fire, become coolness and safety upon Ibrahim." The fire didn't harm him at all. He came out completely safe, a miracle for everyone to see. ESOTERICS The Fire of Persecution The "blazing fire" is a symbol for something far more terrifying than literal flames; it represents public opinion that has been weaponized by falsehood. It is the fire of mass hysteria, collective rage, and a society's complete and passionate commitment to a lie. At its core, the fire was a trial of persecution ( فِتْنَة ). It began with a campaign of lies and slander designed to destroy and tarnish the character of Allah’s guide. The goal was strategic: to ensure that when the final attack comes, no one will rush to his defense, because the fire of rumors has already tarnished his credibility. Everyone will have been convinced that he is a dangerous person. Think of how a rumor spreads like wildfire through a school or a community. It starts with a small spark but, fanned by gossip and malice, it quickly grows into a raging blaze that consumes friendships, trust, and reputations. This was precisely the tactic used against Ibrahim (AS). From Rumor to Roaring Inferno This small fire of slander is then deliberately fanned into a "blazing fire" by the powers that be. The irrational, emotional anger of the crowd is channeled into a single, roaring force. This is the fire of mob mentality, where the heat of blind passion replaces all reason. The very scale of the fire in the story symbolizes this escalation. It wasn't a small, hidden fire; it was a massive public spectacle. The purpose was to make the destruction of the truth a terrifying lesson for all to see. It was a show trial on a grand scale, meant to intimidate anyone who might dare to think differently and challenge the authority of the establishment. The message was clear: "This is what happens to those who oppose us." This pattern of creating a "fire" to consume the truth is not unique to Ibrahim (AS). It is the recurring test (فِتْنَة) that Allah's guides have faced throughout history. We see a perfect example with Rasul Allah (SWS). When he began preaching the message of one Allah, the leaders of Quraysh didn't engage him with logic. They launched a massive smear campaign. They tried to "burn" his message by calling him a sorcerer, a madman, and a liar. They claimed he was only seeking power and wealth. They were trying to create a fire of hatred and suspicion around him so that no one would listen to his actual words. Fire of Political Pressure The fire then escalates to the next stage: political pressure and incitement. This is designed to turn the authorities and the public against the messenger, isolating and crushing him and his followers. We see this with utmost clarity in the life of Maulana Ali (AS). His opponents knew they could not challenge his immense knowledge or piety directly. So, they ignited a fire of political turmoil. They spread propaganda and misinformation, accusing him of falsehoods to stir up rebellion and civil war. They incited armies against him. This "fire" was a deliberate attempt to consume his rightful authority in the ashes of conflict, hoping his message of true justice and faith would be lost forever. In every case, the fire represents the overwhelming force of a society united in falsehood against a single point of truth. And in every case, the miracle of the fire becoming "coolness and peace" symbolizes the ultimate spiritual reality: that no matter how hot the fire of worldly hatred rages, it is utterly powerless to destroy the divine truth, which Allah always protects. Catapulting Ibrahim Into The Fire Narrations state that Ibrahim (AS) was flung into the fire using a catapult ( منجنیق ). The ( منجنیق ) represents the powerful and organized system used to launch these attacks against him. It symbolizes the entire apparatus of the state and the religious establishment working together to publicly condemn him and hurl him into the "fire" of social ostracism and hatred. Let's think about the function of a catapult. It is a machine designed to take a single object and fling it with immense force over a great distance. It multiplies force and broadcasts its payload. The ( منجنیق ) of the story, therefore, represents the mechanisms that amplify and broadcast the lies against Allah's guide. This "machine" is built from several parts: The Pulpits of the Corrupt Clergy: The religious leaders of the time used their positions of authority to denounce Ibrahim (AS) publicly. Their sermons and declarations were the "throwing arm" of the catapult, launching accusations and inciting hatred among the ordinary people. The Tongues of Slanderers: The influential people, the storytellers, and the gossips in the community who spread the lies further are like the ropes and levers of the machine. They create the tension and energy needed to make the attack effective and widespread. The Power of the State: The endorsement from King Nimrod and his court acts as a powerful counterweight. When the political authority affixes its official stamp to the condemnation, it lends the attack incredible force and legitimacy in the eyes of the public. It turns a disagreement into a state-sanctioned persecution. So, the ( منجنیق ) is the entire propaganda machine of that society. It is the coordinated effort that takes the "stone" of a single lie, places it in the bucket of public opinion, and then uses the combined force of religious authority and political power to hurl it with devastating speed and force. Its goal is to tarnish the target's reputation in a "fire" of public hatred, so that no one will dare to side with them or listen to their truth. Despite the terrifying power of this entire system designed to destroy him, the truth of Ibrahim (AS) was protected by Allah, showing that no worldly machine of propaganda can ever extinguish the divine light. The Fire Becoming "Coolness and Safety" ‏ ‏قُلْنَا يَنَارُ كُونى بَرْدًۭا وَسَلَمًا عَلَىٰ إبْرَهيمَ This is the most beautiful part of the miracle. The "coolness and safety" doesn't mean the guides never face hardship. They often face immense suffering. Rasul Allah (SWS) was exiled, Maulana Ali (AS) was martyred while in prayer, and Imam Husayn (AS) was martyred at Karbala. The miracle is that the fire, which is meant to destroy the truth, actually ends up preserving it and making it shine brighter. The truth itself is what Allah makes "cool and safe." Think about Imam Husayn (AS). The goal of his enemies was to extinguish the light of Imamate and the message of his grandfather forever. The fire at Karbala was both literal and symbolic. To the outside world, it looked like the truth had been defeated and burned away. But what happened? The story of his sacrifice became an eternal flame of inspiration. His stand became the ultimate symbol of truth against falsehood. The very event meant to destroy his legacy actually immortalized it. In this way, the fire was made "safe" for the truth, ensuring it would be preserved and inspire people for all time. So, simply, the fire is every attempt to destroy the truth with lies, hatred, and violence. The divine protection is the fact that these attempts invariably backfire. They can harm the messenger, but they can never destroy the message. Instead, the injustice of the attack often ends up revealing the truth for all to see.

title: The Dream To Sacrifice Ismail As
text: Part 1: The Outward Story - A Trial of Absolute Faith The journey began with Prophet Ibrahim (AS), who had prayed for many years for a righteous son. When Allah blessed him with Isma'il (AS), his love for the boy was immense and pure. The most significant test of Ibrahim's (AS) life arrived in a series of dreams—a divine vision in which he saw himself sacrificing his beloved son. Recognizing this as a command from Allah, he faced this unimaginable trial with faith. He approached his son to share the vision, a conversation captured forever in the Quran: Those Who Set the Ranks (37:102) ‏ ‏فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ ٱلسَّعْىَ قَالَ يَبُنَىَّ إنّى أَرَىٰ فى ٱلْمَنَام أَنّى أَذْبَحُكَ فَٱنظُرْ مَاذَا تَرَىٰ قَالَ يَأَبَت ٱفْعَلْ مَا تُؤْمَرُ ۖ سَتَجدُنى إن شَاءَ ٱللَّهُ منَ ٱلصَّبرينَ ‎ ‎ Translation: And when he reached with him [the age of] exertion, he said, "O my son, indeed I have seen in a dream that I [must] sacrifice you, so see what you think." He said, "O my father, do as you are commanded. You will find me, if Allah wills, of the steadfast." Isma'il's (AS) immediate acceptance demonstrated his own perfect submission. With both father and son aligned in their devotion, they proceeded to fulfill the command. As Ibrahim (AS) laid his son down, ready to complete the act, his submission was perfected. At that climactic moment, Allah intervened: Those Who Set the Ranks (37:103-105) ‏ ‏فَلَمَّا أَسْلَمَا وَتَلَّهُ للْجَبين ‎ ‏وَنَدَيْنَهُ أَن يَإبْرَهيمُ ‎ ‏قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ ٱلرُّءْيَا إنَّا كَذَلكَ نَجْزى ٱلْمُحْسنينَ ‎ ‎ Translation: And when they had both submitted and he put him down upon his forehead, We called to him, "O Abraham, you have fulfilled the vision." Indeed, we thus reward the doers of good. They had passed the test. Their willingness was the true sacrifice. Allah declared this a clear trial and ransomed Isma'il (AS) with a "great sacrifice" ( ذِبْحٍ عَظِيم ), which in the outward story was the ram that Ibrahim (AS) was commanded to slaughter instead. The Esoteric Meaning - Establishing the Ranks of Guidance In the esoteric interpretation ( تَأْوِيل ), this divine drama was not about a physical death, but about the establishment of the sacred ranks of spiritual leadership. The Spiritual Paradox At the heart of the esoteric test was a spiritual paradox. Allah had already promised Ibrahim (AS) an unbroken line of Imams from his descendants, specifically through Isma'il (AS). The literal command to sacrifice Isma'il (AS) seemed to make this promise impossible. This created the real test: Could Ibrahim (AS) trust the Giver of the promise even when the command appeared to shatter the plan? This forced him to sacrifice not his son, but his own human logic and his personal understanding of how Allah's will should unfold. The Test for Isma'il (AS): The Worthiness of the Successor The trial was just as profound for Isma'il (AS), as it served to demonstrate his own inherent qualities and worthiness for the sacred office that awaited him. His response was not merely obedience; it was a revelation of his spiritual station. Proof of Infallible Nature: An ordinary person, when faced with such a command, would naturally feel fear, doubt, or rebellion. Isma'il's (AS) immediate, unquestioning reply, "do as you are commanded," was a sign of his pure spiritual nature, free from the flaws and hesitations of the lower self. It was a demonstration of the purity required for the Imamate. Proof of Spiritual Knowledge: His answer showed that he understood the source of the command was divine. He didn't see it as a strange and cruel request from his father; he recognized it as a sacred order he was bound to follow. This points to his own deep spiritual insight. Proof of Steadfastness: He didn't just agree; he actively affirmed his spiritual capacity to endure, promising, "You will find me, if Allah wills, of the steadfast ( صَابِرِين )." This demonstrated the quality of spiritual fortitude necessary for an Imam, who must bear the burdens of the faith and its followers. His willing participation proved that the designated successor was not a passive recipient, but an active partner in the divine covenant, fully worthy of the sacred trust. With both father and son having proven their absolute submission, the true meaning of the "great sacrifice" ( ذِبْحٍ عَظِيم ) was revealed. The ransom was not the ram ; it was a holy rearrangement of spiritual duties. Isma'il (AS), The Ransomed: Having proven his worthiness, Isma'il (AS) was "ransomed," or freed, from the public duties of the Imamate. What does the ransom money do? It frees the captive from the kidnappers. So Isma'il was rasomed, meaning he was freed from the responsibility of being the face of Ibrahim's religion and was elevated to the silent, hidden, and higher rank of the ( أَسَاس ), tasked with being the guardian of the faith's deepest esoteric secrets ( بَاطِن ). His "sacrifice" was forfeiting a public life to protect the very soul of the faith. Ishaq (AS), The Living Ransom: The "great sacrifice" that freed Isma'il (AS) for this hidden role was the public-facing leadership that his brother, Ishaq (AS), then took on. Ishaq's (AS) acceptance of the complex duties of the manifest Imam, guiding the community in its outward ( ظَاهِر ) affairs, was the living ransom, symbolized by the "ram". Think about what a ram is. It's not just any sheep; it is a male sheep, the leader of the flock. It walks at the front, guides the others, and protects them. This perfectly symbolizes the role of the manifest prophet. He is the spiritual leader of the human flock. He is the guide who stands at the forefront of the community, protecting it from error and leading it on the straight path. The physical leader of the animal flock (the ram) is the perfect symbol for the spiritual leader of the human flock (the Prophet). The office of the public leadership for the prophet itself is a "great sacrifice." It is not a position of ease or worldly glory. It is a life of immense burden, constant trial, and complete service to the community and to Allah. The prophet must sacrifice their personal life, their comfort, and often their safety for the sake of guiding humanity. This lifelong commitment of service and spiritual struggle is a far "greater sacrifice" than the one-time sacrifice of an animal. So, when the outward story presents a ram as the ransom, it provides us with a perfect physical representation of the inner reality. The "great sacrifice" was not the death of an animal; it was the acceptance of a sacred, burdensome, and living leadership role by Ishaq (AS), which in turn allowed his brother and his Imam Isma'il (AS) to fulfill his destiny as the permanent, foundational source of all future guidance. This divine plan established a perfect division of labor: Ishaq (AS) became the Prophet leader for his time, while Isma'il (AS) became the permanent, foundational Imam from whose pure lineage the final Prophet, Rasul Allah (SWS), and the universal chain of Imams would descend. The story, therefore, is a sublime explanation of how Allah ensures His guidance for humanity has both a visible, speaking guide and a silent, spiritual foundation in every age.

title: Imam Controls The 4 Ranks
text: To truly understand this concept, you must understand the concept of مراتب - stations. Just as there are physical ranks in this world, there are 4 spiritual ranks. These ranks are called: 1. نبوة - Prophethood This is the broadest rank. It comes from a root word meaning "news," so a Nabi ( نَبِيّ ) or Prophet receives divine news or inspiration from Allah. This is the fundamental divine connection. Many prophets have existed throughout history, each tasked with guiding their specific community. 2. رسالة - Messengership This is a higher and more specific rank within Prophethood. It originates from a root word meaning "to send," so a Rasul ( رَسُول ) or Messenger is a Prophet who is sent with a major initiative. They are given law-giving revelation that establishes a new era of religion for a large part of humanity. This rank is held by the six great Natiqs ( نَاطِق ) or Speaker-Prophets: Adam, Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, Isa, and finally, Rasul Allah (SWS). They brought the sacred law ( شَرِيعَة ) and the scripture, which is the outward ( ظَاهِر ) form of the faith. If نبوۃ has a special connection to the divine headquarters, رسالۃ is being sent out as the lead architect with a brand-new blueprint for an entire age. 3. وصاية - The Foundation of Succession This is the office of the Wasi ( وصي ), which means Legatee or Successor. This rank is also known as the Asas ( أَسَاس ), or the Foundation. The Wasi is the specifically designated, authoritative successor to the Rasul/Natiq. His primary mission is not to bring a new law, but to safeguard and reveal the inner, esoteric meaning ( بَاطِن ) of the Natiq's message. He is the silent partner who understands the true spirit of the revelation. For every Natiq, there is one Asas—for example, Maulana Ali (AS) was the Asas of Rasul Allah (SWS). The Rasul (architect) designs and builds the magnificent outer structure of a palace. The Wasi is the master of the estate who holds the keys to all the secret inner rooms and explains the architect's true, profound vision to those who are worthy. 4. إمامة - Continuous, Living Guidance This is the central and perpetual institution of guidance. The Imam ( إمام ), whose authority is hereditary, is the inheritor of the complete authority of both the Rasul and the Wasi. He is the living guide for his time, responsible for both the outward ( ظَاهِر ) and inward ( بَاطِن ) dimensions of the faith. While a Rasul appears only after long cycles to bring a new law, the Imamate is the continuous, unbroken chain—the "Rope of God" ( حَبْل اللہ )—that is always present on earth to guide humanity. The Imam is the living, speaking Quran, the authoritative interpreter of the faith in every age. After the original architect (Rasul) and the master of the inner secrets (Wasi) have established the palace, a continuous line of hereditary Governors (Imams) is appointed. In every generation, the governor manages the palace, maintains its structure, and reveals its treasures to the inhabitants, ensuring it remains a vibrant, living center. Two Types of Imamate: Now that we understand the four major ranks, the next thing we need to learn is the difference between a Trustee Imam ( امام المستودع ) and a Permanent Imam ( امام المستقر ). Let's first understand these words in a linguistic context. Mustaqarr ( مُسْتَقَرّ ) - The Place of Permanent Settlement The Root: The word مستقر comes from the three-letter root قرر . The core meaning of this root is to be settled, to be stable, to rest, to be established, to be permanent. The Feeling: Think of something that has found its final and permanent home. Imagine water that has pooled and become a calm, settled lake ( قرار ). Or the feeling of coolness ( قرۃ العیون ) in the eyes, which is a metaphor for deep, settled joy and peace. It is a state of stability and permanence. Linguistic Form: The word مستقر is a noun of place. It means "a place of settlement" or "a place of permanent establishment." It is where something belongs and will remain. Mustawda' ( مُسْتَوْدَع ) - The Place of Temporary Safekeeping The Root: The word مستودع comes from the root ودع . The core meaning of this root is to deposit something for safekeeping, to entrust, to leave something with someone temporarily. The Feeling: This is the act of giving a valuable item to a trusted friend to hold onto while you are on a journey. The item is safe and cared for, but everyone understands that the ownership has not changed, and the item will be returned. It is a temporary deposit ( وادعۃ ). Linguistic Form: مستودع is also a noun of place. It means "a place of deposit" or "a place of temporary safekeeping." It is a place where something is held in trust, but not where it will remain forever. These two words are even used together in the Quran to make this very distinction: Cattle (6:98) ‏ ‏وَهُوَ ٱلَّذى أَنشَأَكُم مّن نَّفْسٍۢ وَحدَةٍۢ فَمُسْتَقَرٌّۭ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌۭ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْآيَت لقَوْمٍۢ يَفْقَهُونَ ‎ ‎ Translation: And it is He who produced you from one soul and [gave you] a place of dwelling and of storage. We have detailed the signs for people who understand. The Two Types of Imamate Now, when we apply these precise linguistic meanings to the Imamate, the concepts become perfectly clear. The امام المستقر is the Imam in whom the divine authority of the Imamate has found its permanent home. He is the one from whose lineage the Imamate is destined to continue . The sacred trust ( امانۃ ) has "settled" in his progeny. His authority is hereditary and perpetual . It is not a temporary assignment. He is the ultimate authority of his time. The امام المستودع is an Imam who holds the full authority of the office for a specific time, but as a trust . The authority of the Imamate is "deposited" with him for safekeeping, often to protect the مستقر Imam during a time of danger or to fulfill a specific mission. His function is temporary , akin to that of a regent or a trustee. Most importantly, the Imamate does not continue through his children. After his mission is complete, the authority reverts to the مستقر line. Owner vs Trustee The Imam controls four ranks. I'm not going to go into the specifics of what these ranks are composed of. Cuz that will be too advanced a topic for us. We are here to learn basic esoterics. However, for now, simply understand that the Imam controls all four ranks of the Prophet's Messenger, the legacy, and the Imamate. The امام المستقر inherits the ranks of امامۃ and وصایۃ from his father. The person who holds these ranks is called the Imam. Think of it like this. The person who wins the election is called "Mr. President". Before that, he was just a governor or even a regular man. Similarly, an Imam can have multiple sons, but whoever receives these ranks of امامۃ and وصایۃ becomes the Imam. Isma'il (AS) was the first in the line of the المستقر Imams after the new covenant was established with Ibrahim (AS). His rank was the permanent, established foundation for the entire future chain of Imams. The امام المستودع on the other hand, does not inherit his position. He is given the ranks of نبوۃ and/or رسالۃ, which then makes him a نبی or a رسول . Notice I did not say the word inherit. This is because these ranks belong to the Imam, but they are temporarily given to the prophets so they can carry out their mission. Ishaq (AS) was a المستودع Imam. He held the legitimate authority for his lifetime, guiding the community publicly. But the permanent, hereditary line did not continue through him; it was destined to continue through the line of the المستقر Imam, Isma'il (AS). This is the role that Sam, the son of Nuh (AS), also held.

title: The House Of Allah: A Symbol For The Imam
text: The Spiritual Essence of Hajj The establishment of Ibrahim's (AS) faith, and that of his son Isma'il (AS), is perfected in the raising of the foundations of the Holy Ka'ba ( الكعبة ). They both worked together to construct the Ka'bah. The Qur'an describes them raising the foundations, which is often interpreted as either rebuilding a structure that had fallen into disrepair (poor condition of a building or structure due to neglect) since the time of Adam, or establishing a new sanctuary on a divinely appointed site. As they built, they did not simply perform a physical task; they were engaged in an act of profound worship. They constantly prayed to Allah to accept their work as a sincere act of devotion. They supplicated for themselves and their future generations, asking Allah to make them a community submissive to Him and to guide them in their rites of worship. The final act was the placement of the Black Stone ( الحجر الاسود ), a sacred stone given to Ibrahim by Jibrael to be a part of the structure. The significance of this narrative is immense: A House for All of Humanity: The Ka'bah was not built for a specific tribe or nation, but as the first house of worship dedicated to the One God for all of humanity. Symbol of Monotheism: It stands as a physical symbol of the pure, unadulterated monotheism that Ibrahim (AS) preached and the worship of Allah alone. The Origin of Hajj: The story provides the divine origin for the Hajj pilgrimage, establishing the Ka'bah as the central point of worship and the destination for pilgrims. Submission and Trust: The entire account is a testament to the absolute submission of Ibrahim and Isma'il to Allah's will. Just as Nuh (AS) built the ark on the principles of لا الہ الا اللہ comprising the divine numbers of 2, 3, 4, 7, and 12, Ibrahim (AS) set the foundation of his Shariah on the same principles. So the physical journey of Hajj extends significantly beyond its rituals. Every physical act of Hajj serves as a vital key that opens access to a higher spiritual truth, helping the soul progress back toward its Divine source. This act is mentioned in the Quran: The Cow (2:127) ‏ ‏وَإذْ يَرْفَعُ إبْرَهیمُ ٱلْقَوَاعدَ منَ ٱلْبَيْت وَإسْمَعيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ منَّا ۖ إنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّميعُ ٱلْعَليمُ ‎ Translation: And [mention] when Abraham was raising the foundations of the House and [with him] Ishmael, [saying], "Our Lord, accept [this] from us. Indeed, You are the Hearing, the Knowing. Rituals and Their Inner Meanings The Duality: The physical Ka'ba in Mecca symbolizes a higher spiritual reality within the realm of faith. It represents the Imam of the Time , who is the "speaking Ka'ba" to whom our souls must turn. The physical structure gives us a direction for our bodies to face in prayer, while the Imam guides our souls toward the "straight path." He is the true spiritual direction ( قبلة ) for our souls. Similarly, the most essential act of circumambulation ( طواف ) around the Ka'ba is much more than just circling a stone structure. It symbolizes the soul's orbit around the spiritual center ( قطب ) of the universe, which is the Imam. By aligning ourselves with the Imam, our individual souls, which are like small universes (microcosms), align with the grand divine order (macrocosm). The Imam is considered the "House of Allah" in a very intimate sense, as a Sacred Hadith states: لَا تَسَعُنِي أَرْضِي وَلَا سَمَائِي وَلَكِنْ يَسَعُنِي قَلْبُ عَبْدِي الْمُؤْمِنِ My earth and My heaven cannot contain Me, but the heart of my faithful servant contains Me. Here, the true believer means the Imam. Allah's light dwells within and illuminates the world through the pure heart of the Imam, who is seen as the true Divine Throne ( عرش ). What is Hajj? It is a physical journey that represents the spiritual journey of a believer's soul returning to its true source. The Perfect Cube: Its perfect cube shape symbolizes the stability and perfection of the divine law and spiritual order on earth. This perfection is embodied in the person of the Imam, who represents the complete and stable guidance from Allah. It is considered a perfect shape not only for its physical properties but also for its symbolic representation of balance and completion within the universe. The literal understanding of the cube is that it is considered a perfect solid shape in geometry. It is a body with equal length, width, and depth. It has six equal square faces, Twelve equal and parallel sides, and Eight equal solid angles. From a symbolic standpoint, the cube's perfection comes from its number four (4). The face of a cube, is a foundational element in both the natural world and the spiritual hierarchy. The natural world is composed of four primary components: fire, air, water, and earth (النار، الهواء، الماء، والأرض). These elements, in turn, are associated with the four humors of the body and the four seasons of the year, demonstrating a pervasive pattern of fours in all of creation. The Four Faces: This structure of the physical world corresponds to the four ranks of the spiritual world Allah ( مبدع تعالی ), The Universal Intellect ( العقل الكلي ), The Universal Soul ( النفس الكلية ), and The First Matter ( الهيولى الأولى ). The cube, as a perfectly symmetrical and balanced form derived from the square, is a tangible representation of this cosmic harmony and the flawless order of the universe, linking the physical and spiritual realms through a sacred geometric form. The Four Corners: The four corners of the Ka'ba represent the four major cycles of revelation brought by the 4 great Speakers ( ناطق ) from Ibrahim's (AS) descendants. These are Prophet Musa ( موسى ), Prophet Isa ( عيسى ), Prophet Muhammad ( محمد ), and The final Resurrector ( قائم ). The cycles of موسى and عيسى established the outer laws and hinted at their inner spirit. The time period of Prophet Muhammad ( محمد ) brought a perfect blend of the outer form ( ظاهر ) and the inner meaning ( باطن ) to the laws that موسی and عیسی presented. The two corners embraced by the semicircular wall ( حجر إسماعيل ) signify that the light of earlier revelations has been fulfilled and absorbed within the all-encompassing light of the final message. The circumambulation ( طواف ) continues around the legacy of Prophet Muhammad ( محمد ) and the Imams, leading to the era of the Giver of Resurrection ( قائم ), who will unveil the pure inner meaning ( باطن ) and bring about the Great Resurrection ( قيامة الكبرى )—which is the awakening of souls from ignorance into pure Gnosis ( معرفة ). The Black Stone ( الحجر الأسود ): Touching the Black Stone is a physical representation of the spiritual covenant ( بيعة ), symbolizing the merging of the believer's soul with the divine light of the Imam. It is stated that the stone was originally a pure white gem from paradise that turned black by absorbing humanity's spiritual impurities. This symbolizes the Imam's role: to take on the spiritual burdens of his followers, absorbing their darkness and giving them his light in a sacred, transformative exchange. Seven Circuits of Circumambulation ( طواف ): The number seven is particularly significant because it represents cosmic completeness and perfection. The number seven is seen as "the first perfect number" in our philosophical tradition, bringing together the principles of even and odd numbers to form a whole. Think of it like a beautiful piece of music that is composed of two different notes, one high and one low. Neither note is a complete song on its own, but when they are played together, they create a harmonious and perfect melody. This is precisely how we understand the number seven. The number seven is composed of the odd number three and the even number four. The number three (3) represents the Universal Soul ( النفس الكلية ) and the spiritual realm, which is the inner, unseen reality. Three, because he is the third in the divine realm after Allah and the Universal Intellect [ عقل کل ]. He is the first odd number (3 is the first odd number), often associated with singularity and the inner essence. The number four (4) represents the four elements (fire, air, water, earth) that make up the physical world, which is the outer, visible reality. It is an even number, which is often associated with multiplicity and the outward form. When you bring these two numbers together, they form seven. This symbolizes the perfect union of the spiritual (odd, 3) and the physical (even, 4) worlds. It is this unity that creates a complete and perfect whole. For us, this concept is reflected in many aspects of the faith. For instance, the seven circumambulations ( طواف ) around the Kaaba during the pilgrimage ( حج ) symbolize a perfect, complete cycle of devotion that encompasses both our physical actions and our spiritual intention. The seven verses of Surah Al-Fatiha in the Quran represent a perfect, complete prayer that guides a believer on the path to truth and spiritual fulfillment. In each case, the number seven represents a balance between the outer actions and the inner state, leading to a state of perfection. The idea of the seven circuits ( المدارات السبع ) is an allegorical explanation of the soul's journey. Think of the soul as a pilgrim on a path to reunion with Allah. To get there, the soul must pass through seven distinct spiritual spheres, much like a traveler passes through seven different regions on a long journey. These seven circuits correspond to the celestial spheres, with each one representing a stage of spiritual purification and enlightenment . This journey is guided by the Imam, who serves as the spiritual guide for the soul, helping it navigate the complexities of each stage. This process ultimately leads the soul to shed its material limitations and ascend to a higher, angelic state. This journey is also described with a beautiful architectural analogy. The human body is like a city protected by seven walls ( أسوار ). To reach the heart of the city, which is the divine presence within, the soul must pass through these seven walls. A person who is consumed by worldly distractions is trapped outside the walls, unable to begin this spiritual journey. Only by dedicating oneself to knowledge ( علم ) and good deeds ( أعمال صالحة ) can the soul pass through each wall, shed the darkness of ignorance, and achieve reunion with Allah, who is the ultimate source of all existence. Story Of Hajr The story of Hajr ( حاجرۃ ) and her search for water is a deeply significant narrative in Islam, closely tied to the rituals of Hajj. It begins when Ibrahim (AS), following a command from Allah, took his wife, حاجرۃ , and their infant son, اسماعیل , to the barren and uninhabited valley of Makkah. He left them there with only a small amount of water and dates, entrusting them to Allah's care. Soon, the provisions ran out. حاجرۃ and her baby were overcome by thirst. Desperate to find water for her son, حاجرۃ began to run between two nearby hills, Safa and Marwah, hoping to spot a caravan or a source of water. She ran from Safa to Marwah and back, a total of seven times. With each circuit, her hope was broken and then renewed, but she found no water. Exhausted and distressed, she returned to her son. It was then, as she stood near him, that she saw a spring of water gushing forth from the ground where اسماعیل had been kicking his feet in distress. When the miraculous water began gushing out of the ground, she feared it would be lost in the sand of the desert. She quickly started to build a small basin around the spring with her hands and called out, "Zam! Zam!" which means "Stop! Stop!" or "Gather!" in Arabic, to contain the water. This cry of urgency and relief gave the well its name. The Seven Circuits of Striving ( سعي ): The act of حاجرۃ became a ritual as a symbolic re-enactment of her desperate search for water for both Umrah and Hajj. The names of the two hills, Safa ( صفا ) and Marwa ( مروة ), hold significant linguistic and symbolic meanings that are central to their importance in the Hajj and Umrah pilgrimages. Let's understand their etymology to help us appreciate the more profound, spiritual significance of the ritual of Sai ( سعي ). The name Safa ( صفا ) comes from the Arabic root صَفَوَ (ṣafaw), which means to be clear, pure, or serene. It refers to a smooth, hard rock. In a spiritual sense, this name represents the concept of purity of the heart and mind , a state of clarity and sincerity . It is from this hill that the Prophet Muhammad first publicly preached, symbolizing a call to spiritual purity and clarity of faith. The name Marwa ( مروة ) comes from the Arabic root مَرَوَ (maraw), which refers to a hard, white stone, often a flint stone used for striking fire. Symbolically, this name represents strength, resilience, and action. It signifies the struggle and perseverance required to maintain a state of purity and to act upon one's faith. The linguistic meanings of these two hills perfectly complement each other. The journey between Safa and Marwa in the ritual of Sai is not just a physical act; it is a symbolic journey of the soul. The movement begins with Safa (purity), representing the internal state of the believer. It ends at Marwa (resilience and action), signifying the outward manifestation of that purity through struggle and effort. The seven circuits between them embody the complete spiritual journey of a believer, which requires both an inner state of purity and an outer, unwavering commitment to action in the face of adversity. That is what the act of Sai is a reminder of. This is a profound example of how the physical world can serve as a powerful metaphor for the spiritual realities within our faith. The Striving of Hope Over Despair ( سعي ) This is why the ritual is called سعي , which means "to strive," "to endeavor," or "to make an effort." Her running was like a physical prayer, an outward expression of her inner state. She was acting out the struggle between hope and despair. Imagine the two hills, Safa ( صَفَا ) and Marwa ( مَرْوَة ), as symbols. Safa ( صَفَا ), which means purity and clarity, can represent a moment of hope, a clear vantage point from which she searches for Allah’s mercy. Marwa ( مَرْوَة ), which can be related to a hard, barren flint stone, can represent the harsh reality of her situation, a moment of potential despair. Her first run from Safa ( صَفَا ) to Marwa ( مَرْوَة ) was an act of hope, meeting a stark reality. But instead of collapsing in despair at Marwa ( مَرْوَة ), she made a conscious choice: she refused to give up. She turned back and ran towards hope (Safa) again. Her running back and forth was a refusal to accept despair. It was a demonstration that even when faced with a harsh reality, one must actively strive and keep returning to a state of hope and trust in Allah. The Number Seven Why seven times? Because seven symbolizes completion, perfection, and the entirety of a cycle. They represent a complete and total cycle of human effort. She was demonstrating that she had done absolutely everything humanly possible. She had exhausted every option and put in her maximum effort. She left no stone unturned and no hill unclimbed. This act of completing the cycle of human striving is a necessary spiritual state one must reach before divine intervention occurs. How does this relate to us? It has many connotations: It symbolizes the believer's necessary movement between the recognition of the ranks of the Speaker ( ناطق ), represented by Safa ( صَفَا ), and the rank of his ( أساس ), represented by Marwa ( مَرْوَة ). So, it means the necessary effort a believer must make in recognizing the 7 Nutaqa during this period of concealment. Recognizing the stations of Adam, Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, Isa, Muhammad (SWS), and Qaim. On a more personal note, it reminds us of the struggle required in our daily lives. There are seven pillars of Islam - ولایۃ، طھارۃ، صلوۃ، صوم، زکوۃ، حج، جھاد - Our سعی represents the believer's movement between following the outward aspects of these pillars from the knowledge of the ناطق and learning the deep esoteric meanings of these pillars from the اساس . Hajar's act was not merely about finding water; it was a physical demonstration of a spiritual struggle and a lesson for all humanity. The Miracle of Water Now we come to the most beautiful part of the lesson. After Hajar ( هاجر ) completed her seven circuits—after she had done everything she possibly could—where did the water appear? It did not appear on Safa ( صَفَا ) or Marwa ( مَرْوَة ), the focus of her frantic search. It did not appear where her eyes were looking. The water of Zamzam ( زمزم ) gushed forth from the ground at the feet of her infant son, Isma'il ( إسماعيل ). It appeared at the very source of her vulnerability, at the very place where she had placed her trust in Allah by leaving her child. The lesson is profoundly significant: We are required to put in our full, complete human effort. We must run our seven circuits, striving as if everything depends on us. However, the divine grace, the sustenance, the miracle (the water), does not necessarily come from the direction of our effort. It comes from where Allah wills. After we have exhausted all our own means, we must have complete trust ( توكل ) that Allah's help will arrive, often from a place we least expect. The water was indeed a miracle from Allah, but that miracle came after Hajar had exhausted every possible option and shown her complete reliance on Allah's mercy. Hajar did not know that the water would gush out from under her son's heel. All she knew was that her son was in distress and she had to act. Her running was a physical embodiment of her struggle and her faith. She did not give up after the first circuit, which showed no sign of water. She continued to run back and forth, ascending the hills to look for any possible help. Her completing seven circuits represents the fullness of her effort and her determination. It teaches us that in our own lives, we must strive and struggle to the best of our ability before we can expect a miracle. The water of Zamzam, in this sense, was not a reward for her prayers alone, but a reward for her action ( سعي ) and her trust in Allah. The ritual of Sai today is a reenactment of this profound lesson for all believers, teaching us to never give up, to put in our absolute best effort, and then to trust completely in Allah, whose grace is not bound by the direction of our gaze. Prayer behind the Station of Ibrahim (مقام إبراهيم): To pray "behind" the Station of Ibrahim towards the Ka'ba ( الكعبة ) is a spiritual acknowledgment that the revelation of the Speaker ( ناطق ), symbolized by the Ka'ba ( الكعبة ), can only be truly understood and accessed through the foundation ( أساس ) laid by the Imam. The أساس is the door to the House of Prophecy. Without his foundation, the structure of faith is incomplete and inaccessible. One's prayer is only accepted through this designated channel of authority. The entire Muslim world faces the Ka'ba ( الكعبة ) during prayer, which represents the unity of the community and the singular direction of our worship toward Allah. The Ka'ba ( الكعبة ) is the outward manifestation of faith, the visible House of Allah. It is a symbol of the ناطق , the one who brings the divine message and establishes the outward law ( شريعة ). This law is a guide for humanity, a structure for our worldly and spiritual lives. The door of the Kaaba represents the اساس , because through it we enter the inner sanctum of the Natiq's Shareah and gain the esoteric knowledge of the outward forms he established. The مقام ابراھیم represents the Imam of the time. The Imam is the one who ensures the survival and proper understanding of the Prophet's message. He holds the keys to the House of Prophecy, and without him, the essence of the divine message remains inaccessible to us. The act of prayer "behind" the Station of Ibrahim ( مقام إبراهيم ) is a spiritual acknowledgment that our devotion is channeled through the Imam. It means that while the Ka'ba is the ultimate point of spiritual unity, we can only truly connect with its inner truth through the guidance of the Imam. Without this foundation, the outward form of worship is incomplete and without a deeper meaning. The Imam's role is to preserve the integrity of the faith and to guide the soul's journey toward the true source of divine light. Our prayers are directed toward the House of Allah ( الكعبة ), but the path to that House is opened and authenticated by the Imam. This ensures that our faith is not just a set of empty rituals but a living, breathing connection to Allah, preserved and illuminated by His chosen guide on earth. Drinking from Zamzam ( زمزم ): This is considered the spring of life (عين الحياة). Its water symbolizes the living, ever-flowing Gnosis ( معرفة ) that comes from the Imam. This is not static, bookish knowledge, but a live stream from the world of truth (عالم الحق). To drink from it is to internalize this life-giving knowledge, which transforms the very substance of the soul, quenching its primordial thirst and granting it spiritual immortality—an eternal connection to the Divine. Think of it like a powerful, gushing spring of fresh water. If left unchecked, the water would flood the valley, becoming chaotic and unusable, and could even be dangerous. The basin that Hajar quickly built around the spring with her hands symbolizes the crucial role of the Imam. The Imam is the designated spiritual authority whose function is to serve as the "container" or "basin" for this immense and potentially overwhelming divine knowledge. The Imam organizes, interprets, and channels the knowledge in a way that makes it accessible and safe for the believers. The cry "Zam! Zam!" is therefore a symbolic reminder of the necessity for a spiritual guide who can manage the vastness of divine wisdom, ensuring it nourishes the soul rather than causing spiritual confusion or "drowning." In this way, the story teaches us that a believer must not seek divine knowledge randomly, but must seek it from its rightful source, which contains and protects it. The establishment of Ibrahim (AS)’s faith is consummated in the raising of the foundations of the House, the Holy Ka'ba, with his son and successor, Isma'il (AS). The Cow (2:127) ‏ ‏وَإذْ يَرْفَعُ إبْرَهیمُ ٱلْقَوَاعدَ منَ ٱلْبَيْت وَإسْمَعيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ منَّا ۖ إنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّميعُ ٱلْعَليمُ ‎ ‎ Translation: And [mention] when Abraham was raising the foundations of the house and [with him] Ishmael, [saying], "Our lord, accept [this] from us. Indeed, you are the hearing, the knowing. The physical Ka'ba is a مَثَل (symbol) in the physical world ( عَالَم الْجِسْم ) for a higher reality in the world of faith ( عَالَم الْدِّين ). That higher, living reality is the Imam of the Time. He is the "speaking Ka'ba" to which the soul must turn. The physical structure is the body, while the Imam is the soul. He is the true قِبْلَة of the soul. The act of طَوَاف is far more than circling a stone structure; it is the soul’s orbiting of the spiritual pole ( قُطْب ) of the universe, which is the Imam. By aligning with him, the microcosm of the individual soul aligns with the macrocosm of the divine order. The Imam is the "House of Allah" in the most intimate sense, for as it is said in a Sacred Hadith, The pure heart of the Imam is the true Throne ( عَرْش ) where Allah’s light dwells and from where it illuminates the world.

title: Preface & Introduction To The Book
text: اَلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ مُفِيدِ النِّعْمَةِ، وَصَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَى مُحَمَّدٍ نَبِيِّ الرَّحْمَةِ، وَعَلَى وَصِيِّهِ عَلِيٍّ وَلِيِّ الْأُمَّةِ، وَعَلَى الْأَئِمَّةِ الطَّاهِرِينَ مِنْ أَبْنَائِهِ الطَّيِّبِينَ... Praise be to Allah (SWT), the Bestower of blessings. May Allah send blessings upon Muhammad (PBUH), the Prophet of Mercy, upon his successor Ali, the guardian of the community, and upon the righteous Imams from his descendants... أَمَّا بَعْدُ؛ فَإِنَّا بِتَأْيِيدِ اللَّهِ وَامْتِنَانِهِ وَعَوْنِهِ وَفَضْلِهِ وَإِحْسَانِهِ، كُنَّا قَدْ بَسَطْنَا كِتَابًا لِلْمُسْتَجِيبِينَ إِلَى دَعْوَةِ الْحَقِّ، الْمُعْتَصِمِينَ بِحَبْلِ خَالِقِ الْخَلْقِ، الْمُتَمَسِّكِينَ بِالْعُرْوَةِ الْوُثْقَى، الْمُلَازِمِينَ لِلطَّرِيقَةِ الْمُثْلَى، فِي ظَاهِرِ عِلْمِ الشَّرِيعَةِ، سَمَّيْنَاهُ كِتَابَ "دَعَائِمِ الْإِسْلَامِ"، ذَكَرْنَا فِيهِ حُدُودَ الْإِيمَانِ، وَالْفَرْقَ بَيْنَهُ وَبَيْنَ الْإِسْلَامِ، وَوَاجِبَ الْوِلَايَةِ، وَالدَّلَالَةَ عَلَى وُلَاةِ الْأَمْرِ، وَلَوَازِمَ الْفَرْضِ، وَأَبْوَابَ الْحَلَالِ وَالْحَرَامِ، وَالْقَضَايَا وَالْأَحْكَامَ؛ As for what follows, indeed, with the support of Allah (SWT), His favor, His help, His grace, and His kindness, we have presented a book for those who respond to the call of truth, who hold fast to the rope of the Creator of creation; who cling to the firmest handhold, and who adhere to the best path in the apparent knowledge of the Sharia. We have named the book "Pillars of Islam," wherein we have mentioned the limits of faith, the difference between it and Islam, the obligation of allegiance (Wilayah), guidance to the leaders, the necessities of the obligatory acts, chapters on the lawful and the unlawful, and the rulings and judgments; لِيَعْلَمَ الْمُسْتَجِيبُونَ ذَلِكَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ظَاهِرِ دِينِهِمْ، وَيَعْتَقِدُوهُ وَيَعْمَلُوا بِهِ، وَأَنْ لَا يُخَالِفُوهُ وَلَا يَتْرُكُوهُ؛ إِذْ هُوَ أَوَّلُ حُدُودِ التَّعْلِيمِ، وَأَدْنَى دَرَجَاتِ الْعِلْمِ وَالتَّفْهِيمِ، وَالَّذِي يَنْبَغِي أَنْ يُبْدَأَ فِيهِ مَنْ جَرَتْ فِيهِ رُوحُ الْإِيمَانِ وَالْحِكْمَةِ، وَخَرَجَ مِنْ حَدِّ الظُّلْمَةِ إِلَى حَدِّ النُّورِ، كَمَا يَكُونُ ذَلِكَ أَوَّلَ حُدُودِ الْمَوْلُودِ فِي التَّرْبِيَةِ حِينَ الْوِلَادَةِ، وَتَنَسُّمِ رُوحِ الْحَيَاةِ؛ This allows responders to recognize that, based on the outward aspects of their faith, they truly believe in it, actively practice it, and do not reject or turn away from it. This represents the foundational stage of learning, the most basic level of knowledge and comprehension, which should be embraced by anyone who embodies faith and wisdom and has transitioned from darkness to light. It parallels the initial phase of a newborn's upbringing at the moment of birth, marking the first breath of life. إِذْ إِنَّهُ خَرَجَ مِنْ مَشِيمَةِ الْبَطْنِ إِلَى ضَوْءِ الدُّنْيَا، فَيُبْتَدَأُ فِي إِصْلَاحِ ظَاهِرِهِ مِنْ قَطْعِ سُرَّتِهِ الَّتِي كَانَتْ صِلَةَ مَا بَيْنَهُ وَبَيْنَ مَا لَيْسَ مِنْهُ، وَتَعْدِيلِ أَعْضَائِهِ، وَتَدْهِينِ جِلْدِهِ، وَتَضْمِيدِهِ بِمَا يُقَوِّيهِ وَيَشُدُّهُ، وَتَعْصِيبِ بَدَنِهِ وَشَدِّهِ؛ For indeed, it has emerged from the darkness of the placenta of the womb into the light of the world. Thus, one begins to rectify its apparent state by cutting its umbilical cord, which is a connection between it and what is not part of it. This is done by adjusting its limbs, anointing its skin, bandaging it with materials that provide strength and support, and wrapping its body tightly. لِئَلَّا يَضْطَرِبَ فَتَفْسُدَ أَعْضَاؤُهُ، وَتُوهَنَ مَفَاصِلُهُ فَتُشَوَّهَ خِلْقَتُهُ، أَوْ يَحْدُثَ فِي نَفْسِهِ مَا يَكُونُ فِيهِ هَلَاكُهُ وَتَلَفُهُ مِنْ أَجْلِهِ. To prevent agitation, its limbs should remain uncorrupted, joints should stay strong, and its shape should not become distorted. This also implies that nothing occurs which could result in its destruction and downfall. وَكَذَلِكَ يَكُونُ حَالُ مَنْ لَمْ يُصْلِحْ ظَاهِرَ دِينِهِ وَيُقَوِّمْهُ، يُوشِكُ أَنْ يَنْقُضَ دِينَهُ وَيَهْدِمَهُ، فَقَدْ قَدَّمْنَا لِلْمُسْتَجِيبِينَ مَا يَلْزَمُهُمْ مِنْ ذَلِكَ رَجَاءَ إِصْلَاحِهِمْ وَتَقْوِيمِهِمْ، فَقَرَأْنَا ذَلِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ، وَأَبَحْنَا اسْتِنْسَاخَهُ لِمَنْ رَغِبَ فِيهِ مِنْهُمْ، وَوَضَعْنَاهُ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ، وَأَبَحْنَا قِرَاءَتَهُ لِمَنْ أَحَبَّ ذَلِكَ مِنْ جَمِيعِهِمْ، Similarly, those who neglect to uphold the fundamental principles of their faith jeopardize their beliefs, leading to potential decline. Consequently, we have offered guidance for those open to reflection, with the aim of fostering their correction and integrity. We have shared this information, authorized its reproduction for interested parties, distributed it among them, and made it accessible for anyone to read at their convenience. فَأَخَذَ مِنْهُ وَانْتَفَعَ بِهِ بَعْضُهُمْ، وَهُمْ قَلِيلُونَ بِحَسَبِ مَا قَالَ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ فِي أَمْثَالِهِمْ: ﴿ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ وَقَلِيلٌ مَّا هُمْ﴾ [ص: ٢٤] وَقَالَ: ﴿فَأَعْرَضَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ ﴾ [فصلت: ٤]. Some of them took from it and benefited from it, though they are few, as the Exalted and Majestic said about their kind: "Except for those who believe and do righteous deeds, and very few are they." [Quran 38:24]. He stated, "But most of them turned away, so they don't hear." [Quran 41:4] وَكَمَا قَالَ جَلَّ مِنْ قَائِلٍ: ﴿وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ﴾ [الروم: ٦] وَقَالَ تَعَالَى: ﴿ وَمَا أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ﴾ [يوسف: ١٠٣] وَقَالَ تَعَالَى: ﴿مِنْهُمُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ الْفَاسِقُونَ ﴾ [آل عمران: ١١٠]. And as the Glorious One said: "But most of the people do not know." [Quran 30:6] And the Exalted said: "And most of the people, even if you strive for it, will not be believers." [Quran 12:103] And the Exalted said: "Among them are believers, but most of them are disobedient." [Quran 3:110] وَقَالَ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ: مِنْهُمُ الصَّادُّونَ الْجَاهِلُونَ الضَّالُّونَ، فَقَالُوا: هَذَا هُوَ الظَّاهِرُ الَّذِي نَعْرِفُهُ، وَمَا نَرَى فِيهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ يُفِيدُهُ . And the Exalted and Majestic said: "Among them are those who hinder [others from the path of Allah], the ignorant, the astray." So they said: "This is the apparent [knowledge] that we know from our ancestors, and we do not see in it [what you're saying] anything that benefits it." فَضَاهَوْا بِقَوْلِهِمْ هَذَا قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ حَكَى اللَّهُ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ عَنْهُمْ، الْمُكَذِّبِينَ لِأَوْلِيَائِهِ الْمُرْسَلِينَ بِقَوْلِهِ سُبْحَانَهُ: ﴿ وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ هَٰذَا إِلَّا إِفْكٌ افْتَرَاهُ وَأَعَانَهُ عَلَيْهِ قَوْمٌ آخَرُونَ ۖ فَقَدْ جَاءُوا ظُلْمًا وَزُورًا ‎﴿٤﴾‏ وَقَالُوا أَسَاطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ اكْتَتَبَهَا فَهِيَ تُمْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا ﴾ [الفرقان: ٤-٥]. This statement mirrors the assertion about those whom Allah (SWT), the Exalted and Majestic, described- those who rejected His messengers. As He states, glory be to Him: "And those who disbelieve say, 'This is not but a falsehood he has invented, and other people have helped him with it.' But they have committed injustice and falsehood. They also say, 'These are legends of the former peoples that he has recorded, and they are dictated to him morning and evening.'" [Quran 25:4-5] وَهَذَا سَبِيلُ مَنْ جَحَدَ حُدُودَ الدِّينِ، وَمَا جَرَتْ فِيهِ حِكْمَةُ اللَّهِ لِلْأَوَّلِينَ وَالْآخِرِينَ، وَمَا أَفْسَدَهُ دُعَاةُ السُّوءِ الَّذِينَ ظَنَّ بِهِمُ الْخَيْرَ مَنِ اسْتَرْعَاهُمْ مِنْ أَوْلِيَاءِ اللَّهِ، وَكَانُوا بِخِلَافِ مَا ظَنُّوهُ بِهِمْ، وَخَالَفُوا أَمْرَهُمْ، وَبَدَّلُوا مَا رَتَّبُوهُ لَهُمْ، فَتَعَدَّوْا حُدُودَهُمْ، وَقَعَدُوا بِآخَرِينَ عَنِ الْوَاجِبِ. And this is the way of those who deny the limits of the religion and what Allah's wisdom has ordained for the former and latter generations, as well as what the evil callers have corrupted- those whom the guardians of Allah (SWT) entrusted with responsibility while having a positive view of them. However, they acted contrary to expectations, disobeyed their commands, and altered what had been arranged for them. Consequently, they transgressed their limits and impeded others from fulfilling the obligatory. ﴿ قَدْ ضَلُّوا مِن قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا كَثِيرًا وَضَلُّوا عَن سَوَاءِ السَّبِيلِ ﴾ [المائدة: ٧٧]. "They had gone astray before and misled many and strayed from the right way." [Quran 5:77] وَلَوِ امْتَثَلُوا أَمْرَ أَوْلِيَائِهِمْ وَسَلَكُوا بِالْأُمَّةِ سَبِيلَ مَا حَدَّدُوهُ لَهُمْ، لَاسْتَقَامَتِ الْأَحْوَالُ، وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لِهَؤُلَاءِ وَلَا لِغَيْرِهِمْ مَقَالٌ، وَاللَّهُ يُصْلِحُ بِوَلِيِّهِ الْبَاذِلِ نَفْسَهُ فِي صَلَاحِ مَا غَيَّرُوهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ دِينِ اللَّهِ، وَمَا أَفْسَدَهُ مَنْ خَالَفَهُ، بِحَوْلِ اللَّهِ وَقُوَّتِهِ وَجُودِهِ وَرَحْمَتِهِ، If they had obeyed their guardians' commands and followed the path defined for them, conditions would have been favorable, and there would have been no disputes, whether for these individuals or others. Allah (SWT) will rectify through His guardian who sacrifices himself to correct what has been altered in Allah's religion, and what has been corrupted by those who oppose Him, by the might and power, generosity and mercy of Allah (SWT). وَمَنْ وُفِّقَ لِحِفْظِ مَا بَسَطْنَاهُ لَهُ مِنْ حَدِّ الظَّاهِرِ وَبَلَغَ حَظَّهُ مِنْهُ، وَإِنْ كَانُوا قَلِيلًا مِنْ كَثِيرٍ، لَمْ يَنْبَغِ لَنَا أَنْ نَقْعُدَ بِهِمْ عَنِ الْوَاجِبِ، فَرَأَيْنَا أَنْ نَرْفَعَهُمْ إِلَى الْحَدِّ الَّذِي يَلِيهِ مِنْ حُدُودِ الْبَاطِنِ، وَهُوَ حَدُّ الرَّمْزِ وَالْإِشَارَةِ، وَهُوَ أَلْطَفُ حُدُودِهِ وَأَقْرَبُهَا، And whoever is granted success in preserving what we have explained to him about the limits of the apparent and attains his share of it, even if it is few among many, it is not fitting for us to hold them back from the obligatory. Therefore, we have decided to elevate them to the next level of the limits of the esoterics, which is the limit of symbols and metaphors, and it is the most subtle of its limits and the closest. وَمَثَلُهُ فِي تَرْبِيَةِ الْأَوْلَادِ وَتَغْذِيَةِ أَبْدَانِهِمْ مَثَلُ اللَّبَنِ الَّذِي يُبْتَدَأُ فِيهِ بِالْأَطْفَالِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَاحِ ظَاهِرِهِمْ، لِضَعْفِ طِبَاعِهِمْ عَنِ احْتِمَالِ غَلِيظِ الْأَغْذِيَةِ، وَعَدَمِ اقْتِدَارِهِمْ عَلَى هَضْمِهَا إِلَى أَنْ تَقْوَى عَلَيْهَا أَجْسَادُهُمْ، وَتَتَهَيَّأَ لَهَا، وَتَأْخُذَهَا عَلَى التَّدْرِيجِ، The example of nurturing children and providing for their physical needs is similar to the example of milk, which is introduced to infants only after their evident needs are met. This is necessary due to their inherent fragility, which makes them unable to tolerate solid foods and digest them until they gain sufficient strength. Thus, their bodies must be gradually prepared to accept these foods. وَكَذَلِكَ الرُّمُوزُ وَالْإِشَارَاتُ تُلَقِّحُ أَوْهَامَ السَّامِعِينَ وَتُثِيرُ حَوَاسَّهُمْ إِلَى مَا يُرْمَزُ لَهُمْ بِهِ، وَيُشَارُ إِلَيْهِ مِنَ الْحِكْمَةِ، فَيَتَشَوَّقُونَ إِلَى سَمَاعِهَا، وَيَتَأَهَّبُونَ لِقَبُولِهَا، وَلَوْ أَتَتْهُمْ دُفْعَةً وَاحِدَةً بِلَا مُقَدِّمَاتٍ، وَجَاءَتْهُمْ فَجْأَةً لَمَا ضَرَّهُمْ وُرُودُهَا عَلَيْهِمْ، وَهُمْ عَلَى هَذِهِ الْحَالَةِ، Similarly, symbols and indications ignite the imaginations of listeners and stimulate their senses concerning the wisdom they represent, prompting a desire to listen and a readiness to embrace it. Moreover, if it were to arrive all at once without any introductions and suddenly, this abruptness would harm them while they remain in this state. كَمَا يَضُرُّ الْأَطْفَالَ الْهُجُومُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِالطَّعَامِ قَبْلَ أَوَانِ وَقْتِهِ، وَذَلِكَ مَا حَدَّدَهُ اللَّهُ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ فِي ذَلِكَ فَقَالَ: ﴿ وَالْوَالِدَاتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلَادَهُنَّ حَوْلَيْنِ كَامِلَيْنِ ۖ لِّمَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُتِمَّ الرَّضَاعَةَ ﴾ [البقرة: ٢٣٣]. Just as feeding children with a solid diet before the appropriate time can be harmful, Allah (SWT), the Exalted and Majestic, has outlined this in the following verse: "Mothers should breastfeed their children for a full two years for those who desire to complete the nursing [period]." [Quran 2:233] لِذَلِكَ بَسَطْنَا لِمَنْ ذَكَرْنَاهُمْ بَعْدَ كِتَابِ "دَعَائِمِ الْإِسْلَامِ"، كِتَابًا سَمَّيْنَاهُ "حُدُودَ الْمَعْرِفَةِ" وَثَبَّتْنَاهُ عَلَى حُدُودِ بَيَانِ حُجَّةِ التَّأْوِيلِ عَلَى مَنْ أَنْكَرَهُ، وَالْبَاطِنِ عَلَى مَنْ دَفَعَهُ، وَكَيْفِيَّةِ تَرَقِّي دَرَجَاتِهِ، وَبَسَطْنَا فِيهِ مِنَ الرُّمُوزِ وَالْإِشَارَاتِ بِالْبَاطِنِ وَالتَّأْوِيلِ مَا يَنْتَفِعُ بِهِ ذَوُو الْعُقُولِ، Consequently, we have introduced a book titled "Limits of Knowledge" to those we mentioned following our book "Pillars of Islam." This work outlines the boundaries of interpreting arguments for skeptics, explores the inner meanings for those who dismiss them, and describes the various levels of progression. Within it, we clarify the symbols and meanings, along with their interpretations, for the benefit of those who can understand. وَكَانَ سَبِيلُ الْقَوْمِ فِيهِ سَبِيلَهُمْ فِيمَا قَبْلَهُ مِنَ الْقَبُولِ وَالرَّغْبَةِ وَالْإِعْرَاضِ وَالزَّهَادَةِ. And the way of the people in it was their way in what preceded it of acceptance, desire, aversion, and detachment. وَلَمَّا بَلَغْنَا مِنْ ذَلِكَ مَبْلَغًا، وَجَبَ عَلَيْنَا مِنْ قِبَلِ الرَّاغِبِينَ مَا يَسْتَحِقُّونَهُ، رَأَيْنَا بَسْطَ كِتَابٍ لَهُمْ بِحَدٍّ مِنْ حُدُودِ التَّأْوِيلِ وَالْبَاطِنِ يَلِي حَدَّ الرَّمْزِ، فَمَنْ سَمِعَ شَيْئًا مِنْ ذَلِكَ عَلَى خِلَافِ حُدُودِهِ مِمَّا ذَكَرْنَاهُ أَنَّهُ غَيْرُ ذَلِكَ، وَأَحَالَهُ عَنْ طَرِيقِهِ عَلَى غَيْرِ مَا كَانَ سَمِعَهُ، أَوْ بِدُونِ الْبَيَانِ الَّذِي أُلْقِيَ إِلَيْهِ قَبْلَهُ، أَنْ يَكُونَ سَبِيلُهُ سَبِيلَ مَنْ ذَكَرْنَا مِنَ الْمُعْرِضِينَ عَنِ الَّذِينَ قَبْلَهُ، فَيَسْلُكَ مَسْلَكَهُمْ وَيَقْفُوَ أَثَرَهُمْ، وَيَنْسَلِخَ مِنْ لِبَاسِ دِينِهِ مَعَ جُمْلَتِهِمْ ﴿وَلِيُمَحِّصَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَيَمْحَقَ الْكَافِرِينَ ﴾ [آل عمران: ١٤١]. At a certain point in the discussion, as their desires shifted into obligations, we chose to present a book that delved into interpretation and the deeper meanings that arose from symbolism. Therefore, anyone who hears anything that contradicts these limits or who diverts it from its original course without prior explanation should emulate those we mentioned who strayed from their predecessors. By doing so, they follow their path, trace their footsteps, and shed the garment of their faith along with them, "that Allah may purify those who believe and destroy the disbelievers." [Quran 3:141] كَمَا قَالَ اللَّهُ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ وَهُوَ أَصْدَقُ الْقَائِلِينَ. As Allah (SWT), the Exalted and Majestic, has said, and He is the most truthful of speakers. وَلَوْ عَقَلُوا قَوْلَ الصَّادِقِ الْأَمِينِ، وَوَصَلَ إِلَيْهِمْ عِنْدَمَا شَرَحَهُ مِنَ الْبَاطِنِ لِبَعْضِ الْمُتَّصِلِينَ بِهِ، وَهُوَ أَنَّهُ قِيلَ لَهُ: يَا ابْنَ رَسُولِ اللَّهِ سَمِعْنَا مِنْكَ قَبْلَ هَذَا الْوَقْتِ عَلَى خِلَافِ هَذَا الْوَجْهِ؟ If they comprehended the words of the honest and reliable one [Imam Jaffer asSadiq], and this understanding reached them when he elucidated the more profound meaning to specific individuals close to him, it was conveyed to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah (SWT), we have previously heard something that contradicts this perspective?" فَقَالَ عَلَيْهِ السَّلَامُ -أَيْ جَعْفَرٌ الصَّادِقُ-: « إِنَّا نَتَكَلَّمُ فِي الْكَلِمَةِ الْوَاحِدَةِ سَبْعَةَ أَوْجُهٍ ».
